pattern_description: At 40 Hz the water surface is in the pre-instability sloshing regime. No stable Faraday pattern yet — instead, large amplitude circular sloshing around the container perimeter. The water surface tilts and rocks. Occasional sporadic standing wave patches appear and dissolve.
node_geometry: None stable — chaotic pre-instability sloshing
ai_art_prompt: Shallow tray of water vibrating chaotically at 40 Hz, no stable pattern yet, irregular surface waves with blue glint, liquid motion study, scientific macro photography

pattern_description: At 60 Hz on a large plate, a single straight nodal line or simple curved ring appears. Sand accumulates in a broad curved arc that roughly parallels one plate edge. Pattern is simple and not highly symmetric — this frequency excites a low-order degenerate mode combination.
node_geometry: Single curved arc roughly parallel to one plate edge
ai_art_prompt: Large steel Chladni plate at 60 Hz showing a single gentle curved arc of golden sand, simple and austere, like a sand horizon line, raking warm studio light, peaceful acoustic geometry

pattern_description: Just above the Faraday threshold at 80 Hz (observed at 40 Hz), water surface in a circular dish shows a single concentric standing-wave ring at approximately half the dish radius. The ring oscillates at 40 Hz. Inside and outside the ring, the water surface oscillates in opposite phase.
node_geometry: Single concentric Faraday ring at r ≈ 0.5R
ai_art_prompt: Overhead view of circular dish with water showing a single concentric Faraday ring, 40 Hz standing wave at half radius, blue water with ring highlighted by raking side light, circular perfection in fluid physics

pattern_type: Simple X — two diagonal nodal lines
pattern_description: At 100 Hz a square plate shows the classic diagonal X pattern. Sand accumulates in two diagonal lines from corner to corner. Four clear triangular antinodal regions are visible. One of the most reproducible Chladni patterns at low frequencies.
node_geometry: Two diagonal lines — X pattern
ai_art_prompt: 30cm square steel plate at 100 Hz with sand forming a perfect X, golden diagonal lines, four triangular clear zones, warm amber side-lighting, classic Chladni demonstration

pattern_type: Faraday hexagons — first stable hexagonal lattice
pattern_description: At 110 Hz driving frequency (55 Hz Faraday pattern), water in a shallow square tray forms its first stable hexagonal cell pattern. A regular honeycomb of hexagonal cells appears, each approximately 8 mm across. The cells pulsate at 55 Hz — visible as a coordinated in-out breathing. This is among the most iconic cymatics water patterns.
node_geometry: Regular hexagonal lattice — honeycomb tessellation
ai_art_prompt: Shallow water tray at 110 Hz showing a perfect hexagonal honeycomb Faraday pattern, blue water with hexagonal cells defined by white crests, overhead diffuse lighting, nature's blueprint in fluid physics

pattern_description: At 130 Hz (near C3) a square plate covered in salt shows a clean rectangular lattice with four clear zones. Bright white salt crystals define two perpendicular nodal lines creating a hash-mark pattern. The white-on-steel contrast is exceptional. A benchmark demonstation pattern for classroom use.
node_geometry: Two perpendicular nodal lines — hash-mark (#) grid
ai_art_prompt: Steel plate at C3 covered in brilliant white salt forming a hash-mark pattern, two perpendicular white lines against dark steel, nine white-edged zones, high contrast minimalist scientific image

pattern_type: Faraday squares and stripes — transition zone
pattern_description: At 160 Hz (80 Hz Faraday pattern) the water surface exhibits a transitional pattern between hexagons and squares, sometimes appearing as irregular stripes. The pattern is less stable than the pure hexagonal Faraday and shows dynamic switching between hexagonal cells and stripe domains. Fascinating instability behavior.
node_geometry: Mixed hexagonal-stripe domains, dynamically switching
symmetry: Variable — hexagonal domains mixing with stripe domains
ai_art_prompt: Water surface at 160 Hz showing contested domain pattern, hexagonal cells competing with stripe regions, dynamic instability, teal water with moving boundaries visible, fluid physics in transition state

pattern_description: At G3 (196 Hz) a 30 cm plate displays five nodal lines creating a 2×3 rectangular lattice. Six oval antinodal cells are arranged in two rows of three. The lattice is clean and photogenic. The sand ridge at the central dividing line is taller than peripheral ridges due to maximum antinode amplitude there.
node_geometry: 2x3 rectangular lattice — five nodal lines
ai_art_prompt: 30cm plate at G3 with six oval clear zones arranged in two rows, golden sand ridges defining precise rectangular cells, side-lit to emphasize ridge height variation, acoustic lattice study

pattern_description: At 220 Hz (110 Hz Faraday) in a rectangular container, the Faraday instability produces a rectangular standing wave pattern aligned with the container walls. Regular rows of parallel crests and troughs, with occasional hexagonal patches at intersections. The pattern's wavelength is determined by the capillary dispersion relation at 110 Hz.
node_geometry: Rectangular standing wave grid aligned with container edges
ai_art_prompt: Rectangular water tray at 220 Hz showing parallel rows of Faraday standing waves, rectangular grid of crests and troughs, soft blue water with white crests, geometric fluid physics

pattern_description: At 256 Hz a hexagonal plate exhibits a stunning snowflake pattern. Six radial nodal arms extend from the plate center toward each vertex, connected by a secondary ring at mid-radius. Sand accumulates in 13 clearly defined regions producing a star-within-circle pattern. Twelve peripheral antinodal lobes arranged with full C6v symmetry.
node_geometry: Six radial arms plus connecting ring — twelve-lobe snowflake
ai_art_prompt: 30cm hexagonal steel plate showing a perfect six-armed snowflake in golden sand, six radiating arms and connecting ring, twelve peripheral lobes, cool blue-white studio light evoking ice crystal, sacred geometry meets physics

pattern_description: Middle C (261.63 Hz) on a 20 cm plate produces a simple bisecting pattern. A single straight line divides the plate into two halves with a shorter perpendicular stub, creating an asymmetric T-shape. The pattern is simple but symbolically loaded as the musical center point. White salt on steel produces maximum visual contrast.
node_geometry: Single bisecting line plus perpendicular stub — T-shape
ai_art_prompt: 20cm steel plate at middle C with white salt forming a T-shaped pattern, single strong central line, high contrast minimalist composition, black background, musical physics at its simplest

pattern_type: Faraday hexagonal lattice — dense medium frequency
pattern_description: At 300 Hz (150 Hz Faraday) the water surface forms a dense, stable hexagonal tessellation with cells approximately 4-5 mm across. The pattern fills the entire tray surface. Under back-lighting, the hexagonal lattice appears as a luminous mesh. One of the most photogenic Faraday water patterns.
node_geometry: Dense hexagonal tessellation, cell size ~4mm
ai_art_prompt: Square water tray at 300 Hz showing a luminous hexagonal Faraday lattice, dense 4mm hexagonal cells, back-lit creating blue-white mesh like a stained-glass window of water, macro photography, physics as art

pattern_description: E4 (330 Hz) on a 30 cm plate reveals six clearly separated antinodal regions in a 2×3 arrangement. The five nodal lines are well-defined and create clean rectangular cells. This pattern appears frequently in historical Chladni demonstrations and is often used in physics textbooks.
node_geometry: 2×3 rectangular grid of six oval cells
ai_art_prompt: 30cm plate at E4 with six oval clear antinodal zones in two columns of three, five clean sand ridges visible, warm amber light from low angle, classic Chladni textbook pattern

pattern_description: G4 (392 Hz) on a circular plate produces a six-armed asterisk. Sand defines three straight diameters at 60° intervals creating six equal pie-slice antinodal sectors. The circular plate boundary contains the pattern cleanly. A mandala-like figure with strong emotional resonance.
node_geometry: Six-armed asterisk — three diameters at 60°
ai_art_prompt: 20cm circular Chladni plate at G4 with golden sand forming a perfect six-armed asterisk, three diameter lines at 60 degree intervals, six equal clear sectors, warm gold overhead lighting, mandala of acoustic physics

pattern_description: Concert A (440 Hz) on a 20 cm square plate produces three parallel nodal lines creating a four-band striped pattern. The bands are clean and evenly spaced. Sand ridges are sharp and well-defined. This is perhaps the most photographed Chladni pattern due to its musical significance — it is the universal tuning reference.
node_geometry: Three parallel lines — four even bands
ai_art_prompt: Iconic Chladni plate at concert A (440 Hz), three perfectly parallel golden sand stripes dividing plate into four equal zones, concert-hall lighting, symbol of acoustic precision and musical tradition

pattern_description: At 440 Hz (220 Hz Faraday), water forms fine hexagonal Faraday cells approximately 2.5 mm across. The pattern is dense and covers the entire tray. Under microscope-like lighting, each cell appears as a tiny diamond of light. The water surface texture is reminiscent of insect compound eyes or soap bubble clusters.
node_geometry: Fine hexagonal Faraday lattice, cell size ~2.5mm
ai_art_prompt: Water surface at concert A showing fine hexagonal Faraday lattice, 2.5mm cells glittering like blue diamonds, compound-eye aesthetic, blue-white back-lighting, A440 made visible in fluid form

pattern_description: At 480 Hz a 20 cm plate with lycopodium powder reveals a symmetric 4×4 checkerboard-like lattice with 16 distinct antinodal cells. Eight nodal lines (four in each direction) create a beautifully regular structure. The pale yellow-green lycopodium contrasts softly with the plate surface.
node_geometry: 4×4 checkerboard — 16 cells
ai_art_prompt: Steel plate at 480 Hz with pale yellow lycopodium powder tracing a 4x4 lattice of 16 clear cells, symmetric grid structure, cool diffuse studio lighting, delicate powder precision, green-tinted scientific study

pattern_description: C5 (523 Hz) reveals a medium-complexity pattern with six oval antinodal cells in a 2×3 arrangement. With lycopodium, the nodal lines are pencil-thin — only a few grain diameters wide — giving the pattern an almost blueprint-quality precision. The pattern is perfectly reproducible every time C5 is applied.
node_geometry: 2×3 grid of six oval cells with ultra-fine nodal lines
ai_art_prompt: 20cm plate at C5 with lycopodium forming ultra-fine nodal lines, six perfect oval clear zones visible, yellow-green powder on dark steel, blueprint-quality precision, acoustic art at the edge of resolution

pattern_description: At 500 Hz, a layer of cornstarch suspension exhibits some of the most spectacular behavior in cymatics. Finger-like projections rise from the surface, writhe and branch, then collapse. The dilatant fluid forms transient columns up to 3 cm high. Pattern formation is chaotic but visually extraordinary — no two moments look the same.
node_geometry: Chaotic 3D column forest — no stable 2D pattern
ai_art_prompt: Cornstarch oobleck on vibrating plate at 500 Hz, white dilatant fluid forming dramatic writhing columns and fingers up to 3cm tall, time-lapse single-flash freezing the alien landscape, physics of non-Newtonian drama

pattern_description: At approximately D5 (587 Hz), fine salt on a 20 cm plate shows a 3×4 matrix of twelve rectangular cells. The twelve nodal lines are sharp and the pattern has a distinct cloth-weave aesthetic. White salt on steel gives a striking monochrome geometric image.

ai_art_prompt: 20cm steel plate near D5 with white salt defining a 12-cell rectangular lattice, woven-fabric geometry, black-and-white high contrast, overhead soft box illumination, textile-like acoustic pattern

pattern_description: At E5 (659 Hz) with lycopodium, four thin parallel lines create a five-band pattern. The bands are so fine and sharp they appear ruled with a draftsman's pen. The lycopodium grains in the nodal ridges are visible to the naked eye, forming a wall of tiny spheres along each line.

ai_art_prompt: 20cm plate at E5 with lycopodium forming four ruler-straight parallel lines, five clear bands alternating, yellow-green powder grains individually visible in macro shot, precision acoustic calligraphy

pattern_type: Faraday fine squares — high frequency water
pattern_description: At 700 Hz (350 Hz Faraday) in a small 10 cm tray, water forms a fine square lattice standing wave pattern. Cell size is approximately 1.5 mm — at the threshold of visibility to the naked eye. Under macro lens photography the pattern resolves into a precise square array. The transition from hexagonal to square Faraday patterns depends on depth and frequency.

ai_art_prompt: Small water tray at 700 Hz showing fine 1.5mm square Faraday lattice, nearly microscopic grid of standing wave cells, pale blue water under ring flash lighting, acoustic microscopy aesthetic

pattern_description: G5 (784 Hz) produces a 4×4 lattice with 16 cells on the small plate. With lycopodium powder, the pattern looks like fine woven fabric. The cells are approximately 4×4 mm each. Pattern stability is good and the figure forms in under 3 seconds. A benchmark high-frequency Chladni demonstration.

pattern_description: A5 (880 Hz) on a glass plate with lycopodium, photographed in backlit mode. The glass transmits light so the pattern appears as dark powder lines against a bright illuminated background. Eight rectangular cells in a 2×4 arrangement glow like stained glass windows. pattern_description: At 1000 Hz a 20 cm plate exhibits a rich 3×4 or 4×3 lattice with 12 cells. The lycopodium defines the finest nodal lines achievable without specialized microscopic setups. Cells are approximately 5×4 mm. This is near the practical upper limit of dry-powder Chladni visualization on this plate size.

pattern_description: C6 (1047 Hz) on a hexagonal plate reveals a double-ring hexagonal mandala. An inner ring of six small lobes is surrounded by an outer ring of 12 lobes. pattern_description: At 1200 Hz (600 Hz Faraday) in a small tray, Faraday waves are so fine (wavelength ~0.7 mm) that they appear as a uniform shimmering texture to the naked eye. Only under optical microscopy do the individual hexagonal cells resolve. The pattern looks like frosted glass or brushed metal — an acoustic microstructure invisible at human scale.

pattern_description: A6 (1760 Hz) on a circular plate shows five nodal diameters at 36° intervals — a ten-spoked wheel with decagonal symmetry. pattern_description: At C7 (2093 Hz) on a small hexagonal plate, lycopodium reveals 72 micro-cells in a dense hexagonal lattice — each cell approximately 2 mm in diameter.
